name: Stilt house silhouette
description: A single-storey dwelling lifted clear of the ground on a field of vertical piles 3 to 5 blocks tall, presenting a distinctive two-band profile: an open pile zone below and a compact living deck above. The footprint is roughly square to mildly oblong, capped by a gable or hip roof. A wraparound verandah often rings the deck, sheltered under the eave overhang, with a ladder or external stair climbing one face for access. The pile field keeps the living floor above floodwater, tidal range, soft mud, or sloping terrain, and is characteristic of tropical-coastal villages, swamp settlements, and forest-stream hamlets. The silhouette suits rustic fishing huts, fantasy bog dwellings, and Japanese minka-style raised pavilions.
placement_rules: Pile field occupies the lower 3-5 blocks of the building bounding box; living deck and roof fit in the remainder.
Piles are placed on a regular grid (typically every 2-3 blocks) under the deck perimeter and beneath any interior load-bearing line.
Aspect ratio (length:width) stays between 1.0 and 1.5 — closer to square than the gable cottage to keep the pile grid balanced.
Access is via a ladder or an external stair landing on one face — never an at-grade door, since the ground floor is open.
If a wraparound verandah is included, it extends the deck by 1-2 blocks on all sides under the roof overhang.
Roof eaves overhang the deck by 1 block to shed rain past the verandah railing.
Avoid filling the pile zone with walls — its open silhouette is the defining feature.

rustic.notes: Weathered spruce piles driven into mud or water; plank deck; shallow gable roof; fence-rail verandah.

fantasy.notes: Tall mossy piles suggesting a swamp or bog dwelling; steep storybook roof; lantern under the deck.

japanese.notes: Minka-style raised pavilion; pale plank deck with dark trim; hip roof with deep overhang; wraparound engawa verandah.
